page_content:
# Passage 10:
Hard water contains a high concentration of calcium and magnesium ions. These ions precipitate with soap to form soap scum and with carbonate ions to form insoluble carbonates. Water softening is a process cities use to remove these ions from municipal water.  
Ion impurities can be removed from water by precipitation according to the reactions shown in equations 1 and 2.  
Ca2+(aq) + CO32–(aq) CP-9-1-equilibrium2.gif CaCO3(s)  
Equation 1  
Mg2+(aq) + 2OH–(aq) CP-9-1-equilibrium2.gif Mg(OH)2(s)  
Equation 2  
Both of these reactions are highly pH dependent and require pH values between 9 and 11 for the calcium and magnesium ions to precipitate.  
Alternatively, an ion exchange resin can be used. In this process, water is passed over a bed of plastic beads containing covalently bound anionic groups balanced with sodium cations. The Ca2+ ions displace the lower charged sodium ions according to Equation 3.  
2Na(–bead)(s) + Ca2+(aq) CP-9-1-equilibrium2.gif Ca(–bead)(s) + 2Na+(aq)  
Equation 3  
Neither of these methods removes all the ions from solution. Further treatment is required before drinking-quality water can be obtained.
